Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: mRNA leaves the nuclease and finds a protein. Then tRNA makes a copy of the mRna and sends it out into the cell.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: Process one- nuclear envelope and pores allow things to travel in and out of the nucleus. Process two- Cell membrane protects the cell and also decides what can go in and out of the cell.Process three- osmosis

question: Polymer Investigation item: A student tested four plastic samples for stretchability. Each sample was measured, hung from a table with a clamp and weights for five minutes, then measured again. The experiment was repeated for two trials. The amount stretched was: Plastic A = 10 mm and 12 mm; Plastic B = 22 mm and 23 mm; Plastic C = 14 mm and 13 mm; Plastic D = 20 mm and 20 mm. Based on these data, draw a conclusion about the stretchability of the plastics and describe two ways to improve the experimental design or the validity of the results.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = a valid conclusion supported by the data and two valid experimental design improvements; score 2 = a valid conclusion and one valid improvement, or two valid improvements without a correct conclusion; score 1 = a valid conclusion only, or one valid improvement only; score 0 = little or no correct information. Valid conclusions include: Plastic B has the most stretchability; Plastic A has the least stretchability; not all polymer plastics have the same stretchability; different plastics have different stretchability; or a reasonable conclusion cannot be drawn due to procedural errors. Valid improvements include: provide before and after length measurements; make sure all samples have the same thickness; perform additional trials; indicate how many weights were added to the clamps.
student_answer: a) A conclusion from this data is that plastic sample B is the the most stretchable.^p b) Two ways that the student could have improved the validity of the results are making the plastic samples the same initial size and allowing the plastic samples to stretch for more than five minutes.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: First, the mRNA leaves the nucleus and enters the cytoplasm. Then, it is bonded to a ribosome and it starts to decode the mRNA. Next, the different nucleic acids arrive and match up with the mRNA codons. Last, the amino acid is formed, and the process continues at the Endoplasmic Reticulum where, eventually, all those amino acids make up a protein.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: Cells use osmosis, active transport, and facilitated transport to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane. Osmosis occurs when water and other materials move from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration. Active transport occurs when materials are brought into and out of the cell using the cell's energy. Facilitated transport occurs when materials are brought into and out of the cell using a mixture of energy from the cell and energy from the products.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: Cells move across the membrane with the use of osmosis, diffusion, and homostasis. Osmosis is when the cell determines what is, and is not, allowed to cross the cell membrane. Homostasis is when the cell is both equally stable on the inside and outside of the cell. Diffusion is when molecules are entering and leaving the cell.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.

Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: The cell membrane regulates movement in and out of the cell by monitoring what passes through.  In prokaryotic cells, an operon (consisting of 'switch' and area to latch onto) regulates the transcription of DNA.  In eukaryotic cells, cell regulation can be monitored by the removal of introns.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. question: Polymer Investigation item: A student tested four plastic samples for stretchability. reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = a valid conclusion supported by the data and two valid experimental design improvements; score 2 = a valid conclusion and one valid improvement, or two valid improvements without a correct conclusion; score 1 = a valid conclusion only, or one valid improvement only; score 0 = little or no correct information. Valid conclusions include: Plastic B has the most stretchability; Plastic A has the least stretchability; not all polymer plastics have the same stretchability; different plastics have different stretchability; or a reasonable conclusion cannot be drawn due to procedural errors. Valid improvements include: provide before and after length measurements; make sure all samples have the same thickness; perform additional trials; indicate how many weights were added to the clamps.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: First, mRNA takes the codes to the ribosome to make the proteins. Then, the rRNA codes for the proteins and makes them. Then the tRNA transfers the proteins back to the nucleus. Finally, the proteins are released into the cytoplasm, and the process begins again.

The amount stretched was: Plastic A = 10 mm and 12 mm; Plastic B = 22 mm and 23 mm; Plastic C = 14 mm and 13 mm; Plastic D = 20 mm and 20 mm. Based on these data, draw a conclusion about the stretchability of the plastics and describe two ways to improve the experimental design or the validity of the results.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = a valid conclusion supported by the data and two valid experimental design improvements; score 2 = a valid conclusion and one valid improvement, or two valid improvements without a correct conclusion; score 1 = a valid conclusion only, or one valid improvement only; score 0 = little or no correct information. Valid conclusions include: Plastic B has the most stretchability; Plastic A has the least stretchability; not all polymer plastics have the same stretchability; different plastics have different stretchability; or a reasonable conclusion cannot be drawn due to procedural errors. Valid improvements include: provide before and after length measurements; make sure all samples have the same thickness; perform additional trials; indicate how many weights were added to the clamps.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. question: Polymer Investigation item: A student tested four plastic samples for stretchability. Each sample was measured, hung from a table with a clamp and weights for five minutes, then measured again. The experiment was repeated for two trials. The amount stretched was: Plastic A = 10 mm and 12 mm; Plastic B = 22 mm and 23 mm; Plastic C = 14 mm and 13 mm; Plastic D = 20 mm and 20 mm. Based on these data, draw a conclusion about the stretchability of the plastics and describe two ways to improve the experimental design or the validity of the results.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = a valid conclusion supported by the data and two valid experimental design improvements; score 2 = a valid conclusion and one valid improvement, or two valid improvements without a correct conclusion; score 1 = a valid conclusion only, or one valid improvement only; score 0 = little or no correct information. Valid conclusions include: Plastic B has the most stretchability; Plastic A has the least stretchability; not all polymer plastics have the same stretchability; different plastics have different stretchability; or a reasonable conclusion cannot be drawn due to procedural errors. Valid improvements include: provide before and after length measurements; make sure all samples have the same thickness; perform additional trials; indicate how many weights were added to the clamps.
student_answer: A) Based on the data, plastic B was the most strethible plastic. This is because, on average, it stretched 22.5mm in length. The next highest amount stretched, by plastic D, was an average of 20mm in length, showing that plastic B stretched the most. B) One way the student's could've improved their design is by adding a third trial. This could help make sure there was no faulty data, such as an outlier. They also could've written the amount of weight that was stretching the plastics. Otherwise, for all the & prove they could've been changing these weights, which would've given them 2 independent variables, therefore, making their experiment invalid.

reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: ProphaseMetaphaseAnaphaseTelephase

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: movement reproduction transportation

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: After the mRNA leave the nucleus, there are four other major steps involved in protein synthesis. First, a tRNA attaches to the strand of mRNA. The first codon of nucleotide bases on the mRNA match the codon on the tRNA. The tRNA codes for an amino acid. Next, another tRNA connects to the next codon, bringing in another amino acid. The first tRNA floats away, leaving the amino acid behind. The amino acids bond. This continues, creating a strand of amino acids, until a stop codon is reached. Then the chain of amino acids is released.

reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: Firstly, the RNA strand travels to the ribosomes. Secondly, the Start codon starts the building of the protein. Thirdly, tRNA comes and builds the protein. Lastly, the Stop codon comes along and ends the production of the protein

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: The RNA divides into mRNA and then the mRNA goes out and gives coded instructions to the organelles.

The movement will always go from high concentration of the substance to low concentration. Once the concentration of the substance reaches equilibrium, or is the equal, the substance will move back and forth equally.A specific type of diffusion is osmosis, the diffusion of water. Facilitaed diffusion is used to transport substances that canot go through te semipermeable cell membrane. It uses no energy because it still diffusion. It uses the channel protiens in the cell membrane so that the substance can pass through. Active transport is used when a substance goes from low to high concentration. This type of transportation does use energy because it 'goes against the flow.' It is used when a cell need to take in nutrients or get rid of waste.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.

There is a type of diffusion where a cell moves around and engulfs things and there is a type of diffusion where the cells have both holes and they engulf things.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: Three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane include: Keeping all the healthy cells healthy, getting rid of the bad cells, and maintaining a smooth environment.

Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: The cell membrane moves substances near the nucleaus. As new cells form, the membrane starts to multiply as the cells do.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: mRNA gives a message to tRNA that has what amino acids the DNA strand has. The tRNA brings the RNA to the DNA sequence and the RNA lines up with the amino acids. These make up proteins.

question: Protein Synthesis item: Starting with mRNA leaving the nucleus, list and describe four major steps involved in protein synthesis.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = four valid key elements; score 2 = three valid key elements; score 1 = one or two valid key elements; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: mRNA exits the nucleus via a nuclear pore; mRNA travels through the cytoplasm to the ribosome or enters the rough endoplasmic reticulum; mRNA bases are read in triplets called codons; tRNA carrying the complementary anticodon recognizes the complementary codon of the mRNA; corresponding amino acids on tRNA are bonded to adjacent tRNA amino acids; a new corresponding amino acid is added to the tRNA; amino acids are linked together to make a protein beginning with a START codon in the P site; amino acids continue to be linked until a STOP codon is read on the mRNA in the A site.
student_answer: The mRNA is also known as the messenger RNA. It takes the message of the old codon sequence and copys it down into the new sequence and sends it as a message to the tRNA also known as the transfer RNA. The tRNA transfers the new code to the right sequence. Then, the rRNA (also known as the receiving RNA) receives the new sequence and puts it into use so that it will do it's job.

question: Polymer Investigation item: A student tested four plastic samples for stretchability. Each sample was measured, hung from a table with a clamp and weights for five minutes, then measured again. The experiment was repeated for two trials. The amount stretched was: Plastic A = 10 mm and 12 mm; Plastic B = 22 mm and 23 mm; Plastic C = 14 mm and 13 mm; Plastic D = 20 mm and 20 mm. Based on these data, draw a conclusion about the stretchability of the plastics and describe two ways to improve the experimental design or the validity of the results.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = a valid conclusion supported by the data and two valid experimental design improvements; score 2 = a valid conclusion and one valid improvement, or two valid improvements without a correct conclusion; score 1 = a valid conclusion only, or one valid improvement only; score 0 = little or no correct information. Valid conclusions include: Plastic B has the most stretchability; Plastic A has the least stretchability; not all polymer plastics have the same stretchability; different plastics have different stretchability; or a reasonable conclusion cannot be drawn due to procedural errors. Valid improvements include: provide before and after length measurements; make sure all samples have the same thickness; perform additional trials; indicate how many weights were added to the clamps.
student_answer: One way it would have been good to improve the experiment is listing the steps or procedure properly.

question: Cell Membrane item: List and describe three processes used by cells to control the movement of substances across the cell membrane.
reference_answer: Scoring rule: score 3 = three valid key elements; score 2 = two valid key elements; score 1 = one valid key element; score 0 = other, irrelevant, or incorrect response. Valid key elements: selective permeability allows certain substances to move across the cell membrane; passive transport moves substances from higher concentration to lower concentration; osmosis is the diffusion of water across the cell membrane; facilitated diffusion occurs when the membrane controls the pathway for a particle to enter or leave a cell; active transport uses energy to move a substance across the membrane or from low to high concentration or against the concentration gradient; pumps move charged particles such as sodium and potassium ions through membranes using energy and carrier proteins; exocytosis occurs when a vesicle fuses with the cell membrane and forces large molecules out of the cell; endocytosis occurs when molecules are engulfed by the cell membrane; phagocytosis occurs when vesicles form around large molecules; pinocytosis occurs when vesicles form around liquid droplets; protein channels or channel proteins allow specific molecules or substances to move into or out of the cell.
student_answer: One process used by cells is endocytosis.  In endocytosis, the cell engulfs a particle or another organism by completely surrounding it and turning the space with the particle into a vesicle.  This vesicle can now travel into the cell.  Also exocytosis is used by cells to get certain materials to leave the cell.  A vesicle is created around the particle which is then transferred outside of the cell membrane.  Another process used by cells is diffusion through protein channels.  The directs for a protein channel to be opened up in the cell membrane, and diffusion then takes its natural course of moving from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration until equillibrium is reached.
